key_insight: Adjoint methods and PINNs are not universally better than one another; the winning approach depends mainly on how the unknown parameter is represented.
engineering_relevance: For PDE-constrained inverse problems in mechanics, grid-based fields tend to favor discrete adjoints, while neural representations are more natural for PINNs and for closure or constitutive modeling.
method: Set up both methods from a common inverse-problem formulation with identical domains, PDEs, observations, regularization, and optimizer settings.
Compare performance on unsteady Burgers, noisy Darcy permeability inversion, 3D Allen--Cahn reaction identification, and unsteady Navier--Stokes viscosity identification.
Test a PINN-warm-started adjoint workflow to combine fast reconstruction with adjoint-level accuracy.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports matched benchmarks across four PDE inverse problems and finds that representation choice, time dependence, and cost tradeoffs strongly shape reconstruction quality and efficiency.
watch_item: Fair comparison is highly sensitive to matching parameterization, precision, and optimizer settings, so implementation details can still bias conclusions.
paper_title: Adjoint Method versus Physics-Informed Neural Networks in PDE-Constrained Inverse Problems
